items.title: Search Beyond What Can Be Taught: Evolving the Knowledge Boundary in Agentic Visual Generation
items.abstract: This paper addresses the limitation of visual generators in handling novel or post-training information. Their core method involves introducing "agentic visual generation" by integrating search tools, but they discover naive search is detrimental. The key contribution is the creation of SearchGen-20K and SearchGen-Bench to highlight this "knowledge boundary" problem and demonstrate that current generators perform poorly on evolving, real-world prompts, necessitating a more sophisticated approach to search integration.
items.figure_caption: Representative search-augmented generations from SearchGen-20K , spanning all twelve failure categories identified from 20,840 production prompts. SearchGen-20K captures the production-scale diverse user requests that demand the unbounded, evolving, and deeply long-tailed world knowledge. The world knowledge ranges from entities that benefit from visual shortcut (left), to complex system or scientific procedures that require textual knowledge. The diversity facilitates research in the structural question this paper addresses: which knowledge gaps can a generator internalize, and which require search at inference time?

items.title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework
items.abstract: This paper introduces "LLM-as-a-Verifier," a novel framework that treats Large Language Models (LLMs) as general-purpose verifiers for agentic tasks. Its core method involves using LLMs to generate continuous scores by computing the expectation over token logits, offering fine-grained feedback without additional training. The key contribution is demonstrating that this probabilistic approach unlocks new scaling dimensions for verification, improving score granularity, enabling repeated evaluations, and allowing for criteria decomposition, ultimately leading to better discrimination between correct and incorrect solutions.
items.figure_caption: Overall Performance Results. Our proposed framework, LLM-as-a-Verifier , achieves state-of-the-art performance across coding, robotics, and medical domains: Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%), SWE-Bench Verified (78.2%), RoboRewardBench (87.4%), and MedAgentBench (73.3%).

items.title: MetaSkill-Evolve: Recursive Self-Improvement of LLM Agents via Two-Timescale Meta-Skill Evolution
items.abstract: MetaSkill-Evolve introduces a recursive self-improvement framework for LLM agents by evolving both task skills and meta-skills. The core method uses a two-timescale approach where task skills are updated rapidly, while branch-local meta-skills, which parameterize the improvement pipeline, evolve on a slower timescale. This recursive evolution allows agents to continuously refine not only what they do but also how they improve, leading to more adaptive and capable agents.
items.figure_caption: Four regimes of agent skill improvement. No-Skill : no reusable skill memory. Static Skill : a hand-authored s 0 s_{0} held fixed (padlocked). Single Level Evolve : the task skill evolves s 0 → s 1 → s 2 s_{0}\!\( \to \)\!s_{1}\!\( \to \)\!s_{2} , but the driving meta-process stays padlocked. MetaSkill-Evolve (ours): a branch-level meta-skill m = ( ψ , σ , α , π , ε ) m=(\( \psi \),\( \sigma \),\( \alpha \),\( \pi \),\( \varepsilon \)) co-evolves on a slower outer ring via the same five-agent pipeline that rewrites s s , with no extra model and no extra framework.

items.title: CompactionRL: Reinforcement Learning with Context Compaction for Long-Horizon Agents
items.abstract: CompactionRL addresses the challenge of long-horizon agentic LLMs exceeding context windows by integrating context compaction into reinforcement learning. The core method jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation, enabling LLMs to learn from compressed interaction histories. This approach leads to performance gains on agentic coding tasks by allowing agents to effectively utilize information from extended trajectories.
items.figure_caption: Left: Context compaction allows execution to continue under a fixed context window by initiating a new trace from a compressed summary once the context budget is exhausted. Without compaction, execution terminates upon exhaustion of the context budget. Right: CompactionRL consistently outperforms baseline models on SWE-bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0 under compaction evaluation at both GLM-4.7-Flash(30B-A3B) and GLM-4.5-Air(106B-A30B) scales.

items.title: How Much is Left? LLMs Linearly Encode Their Remaining Output Length
items.abstract: This paper proposes that Large Language Models (LLMs) internally encode their remaining output length. The core method involves training simple linear probes on frozen LLM hidden states to predict the total response length. The key contribution is demonstrating that this length information is linearly decodable from the prompt's final hidden state, even before generation begins, and that these learned probes generalize well to unseen data.
items.figure_caption: Predicting remaining tokens on a short example. The answer to “How old is the son?” has ground truth remaining counts 4, 3, 2, 1 (dashed line, hollow markers). The constant baseline outputs the dataset median at every position. The prompt-only probe is trained on the prompt’s final hidden state and decremented by one at each step, capturing the right shape but with a systematic offset (9 → \( \to \) 8 → \( \to \) 7 → \( \to \) 6). The Remaining Count Probe reads the residual stream at every position; its predictions (6 → \( \to \) 3 → \( \to \) 4 → \( \to \) 1) are non-monotonic, yet it achieves the lowest MAE (1.0 vs. 5.0 and 5.5). Section 4 and Figure 3 show a real example with the retraction-token spike.

items.title: Cortex: A Bidirectionally Aligned Embodied Agent Framework for Long-horizon Manipulation
items.abstract: Cortex addresses the limitations of current embodied agents in long-horizon manipulation by bridging the gap between high-level planning and low-level execution. Its core method involves a novel planning interface that translates semantic plans from a Vision-Language Model into executable subtasks for a Vision-Language-Action model. The key contribution is the standardization of manipulation into 32 skill primitives and the injection of tractability principles during data generation, enabling efficient training on extensive real-world and simulated data.
items.figure_caption: Compared to previous works, Cortex is an embodied agent framework aligning the high-level VLM and low-level VLA on the subtask executability and tractability, enabling synergistic planning and execution for long-horizon manipulation.

items.title: GaP: A Graph-as-Policy Multi-Agent Self-Learning Harness For Variational Automation Tasks
items.abstract: This paper introduces Graph-as-Policy (GaP), a novel multi-agent self-learning harness for variational automation tasks. GaP generates directed computation graphs from a modular skill library, which are then iteratively refined through parallel simulations to improve reliability in complex, open-world robotic applications. Its core contribution lies in bridging interpretable robot programming with the adaptability of model-free policies for tasks with significant object variations.

items.title: MoP-JEPA: Hard-Assigned Predictor Mixtures for Stochastic JEPA World Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces MoP-JEPA, a novel approach to world modeling for stochastic environments. Unlike traditional JEPA models that use a single predictor leading to collapsed predictions, MoP-JEPA employs hard-assigned predictor mixtures. This allows the model to learn a quantizer of the transition distribution, with each predictor head representing a distinct successor state mode, significantly improving planning performance on complex tasks.

items.title: Multiplayer Interactive World Models with Representation Autoencoders
items.abstract: This paper introduces the first multiplayer world model capable of simulating complex, dynamic environments by conditioning on the actions of multiple agents. Its core method uses a large latent diffusion model to learn how to attribute scene changes to specific players and maintain coherence under arbitrary action combinations. The key contribution is enabling realistic, real-time generation of multi-agent interactions, demonstrated in Rocket League, with stable long-term rollouts exceeding training horizons.

items.title: OptiAgent: End-to-End Optimization Modeling via Multi-Agent Iterative Refinement
items.abstract: OptiAgent is a multi-agent framework that translates natural language descriptions of optimization problems into solver-ready mathematical formulations and executable code. Its core method involves dedicated agents iteratively refining the model through a novel multi-loop validation architecture that addresses various failure modes. The main contribution is an end-to-end, auditable optimization modeling process that achieves state-of-the-art performance on LP, MILP, and Nonlinear Programming tasks.
items.figure_caption: OptiAgent: a multi-agent framework with internal feedback loops for iterative refinement of the optimization formulation and generated code.

items.title: Selective Disclosure Watermarking for Large Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces Hierarchical Vocabulary Routing (HeRo), a novel watermarking method for LLMs that enables selective disclosure of embedded metadata. Unlike previous methods, HeRo allows different verifiers to access only specific parts of the watermark based on their authorization level, enhancing privacy and controlling information exposure. This is achieved by recursively partitioning the vocabulary and distributing watermark information hierarchically.
items.figure_caption: Hierarchical vocabulary routing on a toy vocabulary of size V = 16 V=16 with a chunking schedule K = ( K 1 ) = ( 4 ) K=(K_{1})=(4) . At stage ℓ = 1 \( \ell \)=1 , the current candidate set (blue) is partitioned into K 1 = 4 K_{1}=4 contiguous chunks (dotted connectors). Using the level-1 key table ξ ( 1 ) \( \xi^{(1)} \) and message m ( 1 ) m^{(1)} , the sampler draws a chunk (solid arrows) and restricts the candidate set to the selected chunk (green). After the routing stage, the final token (black) is sampled from the remaining candidates using key table ξ ( 2 ) \( \xi^{(2)} \) and message m ( 2 ) m^{(2)} .

items.title: SovereignPA-Bench: Evaluating User-Owned Personal Agents under Evolving Intent, Platform Mediation, and Consent Constraints
items.abstract: SovereignPA-Bench introduces a novel benchmark to evaluate user-owned personal agents, focusing on their ability to uphold user sovereignty. It assesses agents across evolving user intent, platform mediation, and crucial constraints like privacy, consent, and resistance to manipulation, going beyond existing benchmarks that often overlook these aspects. The benchmark provides detailed component metrics to comprehensively measure an agent's performance in these critical areas.

items.title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine
items.abstract: TREK addresses the limitation of policy optimization methods that struggle with complex prompts by using distillation for exploration rather than imitation. It achieves this by identifying difficult prompts, obtaining verified solutions from a teacher, and then using these solutions to expand the student policy's exploration capabilities through a forward KL divergence phase. This staged approach allows TREK to efficiently improve performance on challenging tasks by strategically reinforcing promising solution modes.
items.figure_caption: Summary of main results. (a)–(b): Math reasoning accuracy (avg@16, %) across Qwen3 scales on AIME 2025 and AIME 2024. TREK consistently improves over direct GRPO at all model sizes, with the DeepSeek-V4 proposal source outperforming the self-context variant. (c): ALFWorld success rate with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, showing analogous gains in the agentic planning domain. ScienceWorld results are reported in Table 2 .

The diversity facilitates research in the structural question this paper addresses: which knowledge gaps can a generator internalize, and which require search at inference time?

papers.title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework
papers.abstract: This paper introduces "LLM-as-a-Verifier," a novel framework that treats Large Language Models (LLMs) as general-purpose verifiers for agentic tasks. Its core method involves using LLMs to generate continuous scores by computing the expectation over token logits, offering fine-grained feedback without additional training. The key contribution is demonstrating that this probabilistic approach unlocks new scaling dimensions for verification, improving score granularity, enabling repeated evaluations, and allowing for criteria decomposition, ultimately leading to better discrimination between correct and incorrect solutions.
papers.figure_caption: Overall Performance Results. Our proposed framework, LLM-as-a-Verifier , achieves state-of-the-art performance across coding, robotics, and medical domains: Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%), SWE-Bench Verified (78.2%), RoboRewardBench (87.4%), and MedAgentBench (73.3%).

papers.title: MetaSkill-Evolve: Recursive Self-Improvement of LLM Agents via Two-Timescale Meta-Skill Evolution
papers.abstract: MetaSkill-Evolve introduces a recursive self-improvement framework for LLM agents by evolving both task skills and meta-skills. The core method uses a two-timescale approach where task skills are updated rapidly, while branch-local meta-skills, which parameterize the improvement pipeline, evolve on a slower timescale. This recursive evolution allows agents to continuously refine not only what they do but also how they improve, leading to more adaptive and capable agents.
papers.figure_caption: Four regimes of agent skill improvement. No-Skill : no reusable skill memory. Static Skill : a hand-authored s 0 s_{0} held fixed (padlocked). Single Level Evolve : the task skill evolves s 0 → s 1 → s 2 s_{0}\!\( \to \)\!s_{1}\!\( \to \)\!s_{2} , but the driving meta-process stays padlocked. MetaSkill-Evolve (ours): a branch-level meta-skill m = ( ψ , σ , α , π , ε ) m=(\( \psi \),\( \sigma \),\( \alpha \),\( \pi \),\( \varepsilon \)) co-evolves on a slower outer ring via the same five-agent pipeline that rewrites s s , with no extra model and no extra framework.

papers.title: CompactionRL: Reinforcement Learning with Context Compaction for Long-Horizon Agents
papers.abstract: CompactionRL addresses the challenge of long-horizon agentic LLMs exceeding context windows by integrating context compaction into reinforcement learning. The core method jointly optimizes task execution and summary generation, enabling LLMs to learn from compressed interaction histories. This approach leads to performance gains on agentic coding tasks by allowing agents to effectively utilize information from extended trajectories.
papers.figure_caption: Left: Context compaction allows execution to continue under a fixed context window by initiating a new trace from a compressed summary once the context budget is exhausted. Without compaction, execution terminates upon exhaustion of the context budget. Right: CompactionRL consistently outperforms baseline models on SWE-bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0 under compaction evaluation at both GLM-4.7-Flash(30B-A3B) and GLM-4.5-Air(106B-A30B) scales.

papers.title: How Much is Left? LLMs Linearly Encode Their Remaining Output Length
papers.abstract: This paper proposes that Large Language Models (LLMs) internally encode their remaining output length. The core method involves training simple linear probes on frozen LLM hidden states to predict the total response length. The key contribution is demonstrating that this length information is linearly decodable from the prompt's final hidden state, even before generation begins, and that these learned probes generalize well to unseen data.
papers.figure_caption: Predicting remaining tokens on a short example. The answer to “How old is the son?” has ground truth remaining counts 4, 3, 2, 1 (dashed line, hollow markers). The constant baseline outputs the dataset median at every position. The prompt-only probe is trained on the prompt’s final hidden state and decremented by one at each step, capturing the right shape but with a systematic offset (9 → \( \to \) 8 → \( \to \) 7 → \( \to \) 6). The Remaining Count Probe reads the residual stream at every position; its predictions (6 → \( \to \) 3 → \( \to \) 4 → \( \to \) 1) are non-monotonic, yet it achieves the lowest MAE (1.0 vs. 5.0 and 5.5). Section 4 and Figure 3 show a real example with the retraction-token spike.

papers.title: Cortex: A Bidirectionally Aligned Embodied Agent Framework for Long-horizon Manipulation
papers.abstract: Cortex addresses the limitations of current embodied agents in long-horizon manipulation by bridging the gap between high-level planning and low-level execution. Its core method involves a novel planning interface that translates semantic plans from a Vision-Language Model into executable subtasks for a Vision-Language-Action model. The key contribution is the standardization of manipulation into 32 skill primitives and the injection of tractability principles during data generation, enabling efficient training on extensive real-world and simulated data.
papers.figure_caption: Compared to previous works, Cortex is an embodied agent framework aligning the high-level VLM and low-level VLA on the subtask executability and tractability, enabling synergistic planning and execution for long-horizon manipulation.

papers.title: GaP: A Graph-as-Policy Multi-Agent Self-Learning Harness For Variational Automation Tasks
papers.abstract: This paper introduces Graph-as-Policy (GaP), a novel multi-agent self-learning harness for variational automation tasks. GaP generates directed computation graphs from a modular skill library, which are then iteratively refined through parallel simulations to improve reliability in complex, open-world robotic applications. Its core contribution lies in bridging interpretable robot programming with the adaptability of model-free policies for tasks with significant object variations.

papers.title: MoP-JEPA: Hard-Assigned Predictor Mixtures for Stochastic JEPA World Models
papers.abstract: This paper introduces MoP-JEPA, a novel approach to world modeling for stochastic environments. Unlike traditional JEPA models that use a single predictor leading to collapsed predictions, MoP-JEPA employs hard-assigned predictor mixtures. This allows the model to learn a quantizer of the transition distribution, with each predictor head representing a distinct successor state mode, significantly improving planning performance on complex tasks.

papers.title: Multiplayer Interactive World Models with Representation Autoencoders
papers.abstract: This paper introduces the first multiplayer world model capable of simulating complex, dynamic environments by conditioning on the actions of multiple agents. Its core method uses a large latent diffusion model to learn how to attribute scene changes to specific players and maintain coherence under arbitrary action combinations. The key contribution is enabling realistic, real-time generation of multi-agent interactions, demonstrated in Rocket League, with stable long-term rollouts exceeding training horizons.

papers.title: OptiAgent: End-to-End Optimization Modeling via Multi-Agent Iterative Refinement
papers.abstract: OptiAgent is a multi-agent framework that translates natural language descriptions of optimization problems into solver-ready mathematical formulations and executable code. Its core method involves dedicated agents iteratively refining the model through a novel multi-loop validation architecture that addresses various failure modes. The main contribution is an end-to-end, auditable optimization modeling process that achieves state-of-the-art performance on LP, MILP, and Nonlinear Programming tasks.
papers.figure_caption: OptiAgent: a multi-agent framework with internal feedback loops for iterative refinement of the optimization formulation and generated code.

papers.title: Selective Disclosure Watermarking for Large Language Models
papers.abstract: This paper introduces Hierarchical Vocabulary Routing (HeRo), a novel watermarking method for LLMs that enables selective disclosure of embedded metadata. Unlike previous methods, HeRo allows different verifiers to access only specific parts of the watermark based on their authorization level, enhancing privacy and controlling information exposure. This is achieved by recursively partitioning the vocabulary and distributing watermark information hierarchically.
papers.figure_caption: Hierarchical vocabulary routing on a toy vocabulary of size V = 16 V=16 with a chunking schedule K = ( K 1 ) = ( 4 ) K=(K_{1})=(4) . At stage ℓ = 1 \( \ell \)=1 , the current candidate set (blue) is partitioned into K 1 = 4 K_{1}=4 contiguous chunks (dotted connectors). Using the level-1 key table ξ ( 1 ) \( \xi^{(1)} \) and message m ( 1 ) m^{(1)} , the sampler draws a chunk (solid arrows) and restricts the candidate set to the selected chunk (green). After the routing stage, the final token (black) is sampled from the remaining candidates using key table ξ ( 2 ) \( \xi^{(2)} \) and message m ( 2 ) m^{(2)} .

papers.title: SovereignPA-Bench: Evaluating User-Owned Personal Agents under Evolving Intent, Platform Mediation, and Consent Constraints
papers.abstract: SovereignPA-Bench introduces a novel benchmark to evaluate user-owned personal agents, focusing on their ability to uphold user sovereignty. It assesses agents across evolving user intent, platform mediation, and crucial constraints like privacy, consent, and resistance to manipulation, going beyond existing benchmarks that often overlook these aspects. The benchmark provides detailed component metrics to comprehensively measure an agent's performance in these critical areas.

papers.title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine
papers.abstract: TREK addresses the limitation of policy optimization methods that struggle with complex prompts by using distillation for exploration rather than imitation. It achieves this by identifying difficult prompts, obtaining verified solutions from a teacher, and then using these solutions to expand the student policy's exploration capabilities through a forward KL divergence phase. This staged approach allows TREK to efficiently improve performance on challenging tasks by strategically reinforcing promising solution modes.
papers.figure_caption: Summary of main results. (a)–(b): Math reasoning accuracy (avg@16, %) across Qwen3 scales on AIME 2025 and AIME 2024. TREK consistently improves over direct GRPO at all model sizes, with the DeepSeek-V4 proposal source outperforming the self-context variant. (c): ALFWorld success rate with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, showing analogous gains in the agentic planning domain. ScienceWorld results are reported in Table 2 .
